selector.exact: it is in the conflict of these two form-ideals that the contrast of two worlds is first manifested

annotations.anchor: the conflict of these two form-ideals … the contrast of two worlds
annotations.note: The chapter's organizing opposition: the Act (the nude, the deed-in-the-body) versus the Portrait. The first shows an existence in its outward, bounded structure — the Apollinian way; the second makes the inner soul speak — the Faustian way. Hold this pair (Act / Portrait) and you have the whole chapter: two arts, two souls, two ways of being a self.

selector.exact: found the most genuine, the only exhaustive, expression of his life-feeling in the Portrait

annotations.anchor: Faustian man … found … expression of his life-feeling in the Portrait
annotations.note: The core contrast. Classical man exhausts the human being in the bounding surfaces of the nude statue — the soma, the present body. Faustian man finds his fullest expression in the Portrait, which reaches behind the surface to a biography and a soul. Spengler calls the Hellenic nude 'the great exceptional case'; for him the portrait, not the statue, is the natural human image.

selector.exact: But, for us, the portrait is musical.

annotations.anchor: But, for us, the portrait is musical.
annotations.note: The chapter's most beautiful equation, tying this chapter to the last: the Western portrait — 'the look, the play of the mouth, the pose of head and hands' — is 'a fugue of the subtlest meaning, a composition of many voices'. Painting and music meet again. Notice how the claim turns on the prior assumption that all Faustian arts share one inner language; granted that, the portrait simply is music in another medium.

selector.exact: In China the face is like a landscape

annotations.anchor: In China the face is like a landscape
annotations.note: A four-Culture comparison of the human face in art. The Magian mosque 'had no face' (hence Islamic iconoclasm drove the portrait out into arabesque); in Egypt the statue's face was a pylon, a mighty emergence from the stone-mass; in China the face is read 'like a landscape', full of little signs; for the West it is musical. Each Culture meets the face as its prime-symbol dictates.

selector.exact: The Classical statue is a piece of present

annotations.anchor: The Classical statue is a piece of present “Nature” and nothing besides
annotations.note: A compact restatement of the Apollinian soul through its art: the Classical statue is 'a piece of present Nature and nothing besides' — it belongs wholly to the world-as-nature, the bounded body in the present, with no inner history. 'Classical poetry is statuary in verse.' This is why, for Spengler, the Greek lives 'point-wise, ahistorically, somatically' and never produced genuine self-criticism or self-portrait.

selector.exact: as a centre of the World-as-History

annotations.anchor: the portrait … takes its man … as a centre of the World-as-History
annotations.note: The portrait, Spengler says, takes its subject not as a centre of the World-as-Nature (as the statue does) but as a centre of the World-as-History — a being with a past and a future, woken 'out of the stone from about 1200' and 'become completely music in the 17th Century'. The art-contrast of this chapter is the same world-as-history / world-as-nature distinction that opened the book, now read off paint and marble.

selector.exact: a self-portrait is a historical confession

annotations.anchor: a self-portrait is a historical confession
annotations.note: One of the chapter's richest threads: Spengler ties the Western portrait to the sacrament of Confession. A self-portrait by Rembrandt, like a Beethoven quartet or a Goethe autobiography, is a laying-bare of the inner history of a self before a judge — and even the Protestant or freethinker who refuses the priest, he says, still confesses, to himself or to all the world. The Faustian portrait is the picture-form of the confessing, historical soul.

selector.exact: So are the portraits of Rembrandt and the music of Beethoven

annotations.anchor: the portraits of Rembrandt and the music of Beethoven
annotations.note: It is analyses like this one — reading a whole culture-soul off the style of its art — that H. Stuart Hughes judged the durable core of Spengler's achievement, as against the discredited prophecy of dates. Hughes reads Spengler less as a forecaster than as a morphologist of cultural style; the Act/Portrait contrast, drawing Rembrandt's portraits and Beethoven's music into one Faustian confession, is that morphology at its most suggestive — and least falsifiable.

selector.exact: long after the plastic art itself had become entirely alien to our inner soul

annotations.anchor: long after the plastic art itself had become entirely alien to our inner soul
annotations.note: A claim that quietly reframes how you should read the whole Western tradition. Spengler argues that the Renaissance's reverence for Classical sculpture made us misjudge our own art — we kept ranking the nude statue highest 'long after' plastic had gone dead for the Faustian soul. The difficulty: this lets him treat the entire Renaissance valuation of Classical art as a kind of collective error. Powerful, but it assumes exactly the soul-typology it is meant to illustrate.
